title: Assumptions, Principles & the Cost Constraint
event: Delgado Co. purchased land in 1998 for $100,000. A current appraisal values it at $480,000. Determine the amount at which the land is reported on today's balance sheet.

computationPaths.narration: The historical cost (measurement) principle: land is carried at acquisition cost — verifiable and neutral — and is never depreciated or written up.

memorize.body: Assumptions: Economic entity, Going concern, Monetary unit, Periodicity. Principles: Measurement (historical cost / fair value), Revenue recognition, Expense recognition, Full disclosure. Constraint: cost-benefit.
Going concern is what JUSTIFIES historical cost — a business not facing liquidation doesn't need exit prices on operating assets.
Land appreciates in the real world and never on the balance sheet. Gains wait for a sale.

questions.prompt: Land bought for $100,000 in 1998, appraised at $480,000 today. Balance sheet amount = ?

distractors.feedback: That's fair value. Land follows the historical cost principle — no write-ups, no depreciation.
Averaging cost and appraisal has no basis in GAAP — historical cost holds at 100,000.
